dek: Fort Wayne's incomes sit well below the wealthy comparison counties, yet its public realm is one of the strongest in the pilot — proof that a community can choose to build common wealth without first getting rich.

sections.heading: A city building in public
body: Allen County scores about 53.5 on the Civic Wealth Index — squarely in the middle of the pilot — and how it gets there is the whole point. The median household income here is $68,839, comfortably below the affluent suburban counties we also scored. On private wealth alone, Allen County would look ordinary. Its public wealth does not.

Over the last decade Fort Wayne has poured roughly a billion dollars into its downtown and riverfront, and you can stand in the result. Promenade Park anchors a reclaimed riverfront; the Electric Works campus turned a shuttered GE plant into a mixed-use district; Parkview Field brought downtown baseball to Harrison Square; the Rivergreenway and urban trail system knit it together. That record shows up in the numbers: the investment-trend score lands at 88, and the town-center-quality assessment at 78. This is a place actively investing in the things everyone owns.

sections.heading: Where the public wealth leads
body: The strongest single asset is one many cities let quietly decline: the public library. The Allen County Public Library logged about 1.8 million in-person visits against a service population near 385,000 — roughly 4.7 visits per resident, second-most in Indiana. Parks back it up, with about 10.8 acres per thousand residents across some 89 parks and 2,840 acres, and 62 percent of residents within a ten-minute walk of one.

The civic and cultural fabric is deeper than the county's size suggests. Downtown is genuinely walkable, and the arts core — the Embassy Theatre, the Philharmonic, the Museum of Art, the growing Arts Campus — is unusual for a metro this size. Seventy-nine National Register listings, including two National Historic Landmarks, give it real historical bones. These are the assets that make a place worth staying in, and Allen County has been adding to them, not spending them down.

sections.heading: The honest caveat
body: Fairness demands a note we did not field-survey ourselves, so read it lightly: this is momentum on a modest base. The billion dollars is recent, and much of it is a decade old at most. The stock of civic wealth is still building — it is not yet deep the way a century-old endowment of parks and institutions would be. The tree canopy tells that story honestly, slipping from 29 percent to 23 percent over the last decade under development, storms, and the emerald ash borer, with the parks department now replanting to recover it.

Health and safety is where the private-wealth ceiling still presses. Life expectancy is 75.6 years, below where the public investment might suggest, and the child-wellbeing composite sits at 58, weighed down by elevated adult obesity, smoking, and overdose rates. Roads are modeled at about 18 percent in poor condition, and sidewalk coverage thins fast outside the urban core. Public building has not yet fully translated into public health, and that gap is real.

sections.heading: The lesson of the gap
body: Allen County's public wealth runs well ahead of its private wealth — the kind of gap that, in most of America, runs the other way. Rich places often coast on inherited private prosperity while letting the commons erode; Allen County is doing the harder, better thing, choosing to build shared assets faster than its incomes alone would predict.

That is why this profile matters to the index as a whole. Civic wealth is not just a function of how much money residents make. A community of modest means can decide that the riverfront, the library, the ballpark, and the trail are worth building — and can build them. Allen County is not the richest place we scored. It may be one of the clearest examples of a place stewarding what it holds in common, and pointing the trend up.
